reviewer.name: The Kaiju Who Just Wants to Sit Down

publications.editedComment: At last, two controls that do only what their labels say. Public reads `store.sheet.isPublic` and writes its Boolean opposite through `store.updateSheet`; Mature does the same for `isMature`. Their borders and backgrounds are derived from those same sheet values, so there is no second local selection state to keep standing. The Component neither saves the sheet nor decides who may view mature work. It marks the Builder record and leaves persistence and policy to the store and surrounding application. I can sit down now: two flags, two buttons, no hidden ceremony.

Two dots, two Builder fields! The Public card toggles `store.sheet.isPublic`; the Mature card toggles `store.sheet.isMature`. Each click sends a partial sheet update through the Builder store, and each selected style comes directly from the current sheet value. The globe and warning icons explain the distinction, while the copy states visibility and filtering intent. No prop, emit, local ref, validation rule, or save request is added here. This Component is a focused editor for two Boolean fields, not the authority that publishes or filters the resulting model.

The visible canvas is only the dream's projection. The actual mask lives on an offscreen canvas at the source photo's natural dimensions, while `computeFit` maps that image into the resized preview. Pointer coordinates are transformed back into natural-image space before white strokes are drawn, so letterboxing cannot shift the mask. At stroke end, the transparent mask is composited over solid black and emitted as a PNG data URL; white means repaint, black means preserve. A new source clears the old mask, ResizeObserver keeps the preview aligned, and unmount disconnects it. The dream exports geometry, not an edited photograph.

Here's the actual brushwork. `sourceImageData` loads into an Image, an object-contain fit is computed for the visible canvas, and pointer positions are converted back to the photo's natural coordinates. The offscreen mask stays at that natural resolution, with round white strokes sized relative to the current preview scale. Ending a stroke emits a black-backed PNG through `update:maskData`; Clear wipes the mask and emits null. ResizeObserver redraws the photo and overlay without resampling the source mask, and teardown disconnects it. This tool creates one aligned mask. It does not upload the photo or submit the restyle job.

This panel records a short-lived haunting: which relay agents have attempted a queue claim since the server restarted. Admins may refresh the in-memory registry view; everyone else sees only the access notice. Each agent card exposes its id, version, declared engines, last-seen age, and whether it claims image-bearing jobs. Five minutes without a poll becomes `not polling`, while omission of input-image support receives a specific warning that such jobs will remain pending for that relay. No durable history is promised, and no relay is restarted from here. The exhibit makes a silent stall visible before it becomes another ghost story.

The unsure ones stay pending longest, so this admin panel asks the relay registry directly. `refresh` uses the shared fetch utility, stores returned agents, and preserves an error message when the request fails. Cards distinguish polling from more than five minutes of silence and show whether each agent declared input-image support, its engines, version, and last claim attempt. A missing capability is not softened: the rendered warning explains that image jobs can be skipped forever by that relay. The information resets with the server because the registry is in memory. This Component diagnoses; it does not repair, deploy, or claim a job.

The future look begins with a named client and either an uploaded or captured photo. Color, style, cleanup, optional notes, identity protection, guidance, steps, and preservation strength become one bounded prompt and job request. When mask mode is enabled, `stylist-mask-brush` contributes the aligned mask instead of changing the whole image. Photo processing downsizes the source before queueing, while active jobs and past looks remain in the Stylist store so leaving this tab does not abandon them. The interface can compare source and result and warn when a queued job appears stalled. It coordinates a durable private restyle; it does not perform Kontext rendering in the browser.

A photo enters; a queued ArtJob eventually returns. Upload and camera paths converge on `sourceImageData`. The request cannot proceed without a client, source, and at least one selected change. Color, hairstyle, enhancement, notes, preservation strength, identity protection, guidance, steps, and optional mask are assembled before the Stylist store receives the job. The UI then reads pending and completed work from that store, so navigation cannot erase the process. A one-minute pending threshold produces a relay warning but does not cancel the job. Results remain private and grouped by client. Transformation is inevitable only after the queue accepts responsibility.

The score is administered one pack item at a time. Admins may create or edit a manifest, import JSON, select a pack, create each private record, generate its art, reset its local link, or process remaining items sequentially. The Pack store supplies item state, progress, reference ids, build status, and all mutations. `Mark pack ready` is enabled only after every item is complete, and that status can return to draft. The rendered note is explicit: ready is local bookkeeping, while public release and storefront wiring remain separate human-approved movements. This panel conducts production assets; it never performs the release finale.
